## [00:00 - 03:03]

What I'm going to share with you today is something that I shared recently in a workshop around the whole strategy of making Facebook ads work. The actual model of success with Facebook ads, I think it's fairly simple. And I hope you do once you see this model as well. Now, when I look at Facebook, one thing that I often see is that people get confused between the difference between Facebook and a user. And I'll explain what that means just shortly. But if you look at it right now, there are three players when it comes to Facebook marketing. And understanding those three and what those three need, I think are one of the most important things of getting your advertising working. Right. So first of all, there's you. There's you, the advertiser, that you're trying to reach this person here, which is the user. I call it user because that's the term that Facebook use. But this is your customer, your prospect, et cetera. So you want to reach this person here. But guess what? There's a gatekeeper. Right. So that's Facebook. So Facebook are here. And the problem is that, you know, there's two problems I see. Number one is the technical Facebook marketer will do all they can to please the gatekeeper. Facebook is a gatekeeper. So if you imagine you're walking up to a bridge, Facebook is in front of that bridge and it won't let you get to the other side unless you get past Facebook. So the first thing here is you need to know how to please Facebook. Then you need to know. So if you get past Facebook and you get in front of the user and obviously at this point you can serve your I.O.B. How do you get your message through to that person? Now there's a value exchange. So here you need to be passing off enough value and that value has to be high enough for that user to take action. So here there's a value going that way. And here there is a cost that comes back. So I'll explain what that means shortly, but you know, obviously now you're paying for your ads. So that's the most basic part of it. So Facebook send you a cost. You send the user value. If that value is high enough, then that user takes action, right? So the action could be a click, could be landing on your site and purchasing whatever the ultimate action is or the initial action, that's what you want the user to take, right? So if there's enough value, then the user will take action. So the question, first of all, is what do I determine to be value? What is that thing that's going to connect the user to my key action? So the value here is an exchange that's higher than what you're asking for.

## [03:03 - 06:03]

So if you're asking the user to take a click, is that exchange that you're giving me enough to warrant that? So the analogy I often use is if you go back thousands and thousands of years before money was a thing and before gold coins and silver coins were a thing, that value was determined by an exchange of goods. And that creates a valuable exchange. Now for us, I think it's fairly simple and it comes down to the offer. So if you know about the core four, then you'll know that there's four parts of creating an advertising campaign on Facebook, outside of Facebook, doesn't matter. So the first one is your product. Second part is your audience. Third part is your offer. And the fourth part is your funnel. So if we pull out offer here, so we say, right, what exactly is the offer? The offer is the bridge between your products and your audience. So the first thing is, you know, if you've qualified your product and you really understand where the need comes from and where the pain point is or where the new opportunity is for your audience, then you can convert that into a message that's going to work. So your product here could be a Recom store, it could be a service, it could be whatever, even an email newsletter could be a product and you want people to sign up for it. And the audience is defining right, who is that? So you know, if I'm selling a product and it's related to pets and let's say it's something that helps to stop your pet chewing the furniture or something like that. So that's your product. But then thinking about whether it's a pain point or an opportunity. So I would argue that it's solving a pain point. So then that becomes your key angle for the product. So who's the audience? Obviously pet owners probably need to go a step deeper and say, right, it's owners or dogs that also have this chewing problem. So now all of a sudden you've matched your product and audience and you can build an offer. So the offer here is going to be built on solving a pain point. So the offer is all about can I connect my product to my audience? So the user is your audience. The product is this thing here that you're trying to push through Facebook to your user. So now, okay, we've got this construct of an offer. So we know that we want to push this message to this customer. That's when we then build our avatar. So I use the 5W avatar and you may or may not use the same. But the fact is, when you understand your avatar and their needs, then you can construct an offer that's going to appeal to them.

## [09:06 - 12:07]

So assuming you've got the pixel installed, the pixel is collecting data. So did that user click? Did they stay? How long did they stay? How many pages did they visit? What pages do they visit? Facebook's collecting all this data because it wants to know, are you giving this user value? So not only do you need to be thinking about giving the user value, Facebook is also keeping an eye on that. So I'm going to draw the eye of Facebook here and it's kind of just beaming out towards looking at what you're doing and it's observing. It's looking at whether you're serving the user value in the newsfeed and then also through your funnel, whether you're doing the right thing to keep the user happy because the thing is there's an exchange here that Facebook also wants to give the user value. If the user doesn't get value from Facebook, then guess what? They're not going to be on Facebook. So that's the key link between all three of these is that you need to give the user value. Facebook will give you a cost and Facebook will look at the value that it's giving to the user because if you, the advertiser, are not giving value here, Facebook need to either charge you more as a penalty or stop serving your ads. So for example, you could just ban your ad account if it's that bad and this is the kind of value exchange that happens. So the question is, how do you get that value up? That value is increased by your funnel. First of all, reducing your page bounce rate. Secondly, increasing in the site speed so that people are able to get through your lander and your funnel quickly. And thirdly, increasing your ultimate conversion. So whether that's purchase or registration complete or whatever your end goal is, all of that data gets passed back to Facebook. So all of the actions that happen on your site. So that's then what Facebook is looking at to grade the value that you're creating. So everything that we use in our strategy is the BPM method. It really solves the problem. It's amazing, et cetera. Often the business owner understands the audience, you know, that really puts some thought into it. But here comes the problem. Often the offer is poor and the funnel is poor. What does that mean? That means that if you were to push this into Facebook right now, it would not work because you don't have enough value for the user and you don't have enough value for Facebook.

So the offer is we've talked about starts off with the 5W avatar. And the cool thing about this is once you've created your avatar, you know, you have your ad angle, you can create copy and headlines based on these trigger points. You can create your body copy based on the functional benefits and the reasons why someone wouldn't buy. And then your creative just needs to match that. So the, you know, one of the worst mistakes I see, and also some prominent Facebook ads people that also do coaching and training and some e-com people in the problem I see is when they create an ad, and you know, they've got it in their mind that the image has to stand out in the newsfeed, which, you know, the basic level it does. If it doesn't match the ad and the copy, then that creates dissidence. It's the opposite of congruence. So congruence is a big keyword that often comes about when we talk about how to get that value to the user and how to match that user value in the funnel. So congruence is it flows seamlessly, it makes sense, and it just, it's no effort for me in terms of my thinking. So there's no cognitive effort. I see this, I get it, I click it, I learn on the landing page, I get it, I'm going to

## [15:08 - 18:09]

click and I'm going to buy it. So that's congruence all the way through. So here, for example, in my dog toy example, you have a problem, your dog choose on your furniture, here's a solution, here's how it works, maybe it's a video, maybe it's a static image. You click, the landing page talks about exactly the same thing, and it expands further to break away your questions and concerns. That's a congruent funnel. Dissidence is created when you have a really good ad angle, for example, copy, your target is good, but someone has taught you that the image just needs to stand out and you just put a random image in there. So let's say for this dog toy example, your image is a random picture of, I don't know, a dog that's got, let's say it's a golden Labrador and it's been dyed pink or something like that. Like, well, that doesn't even make sense. Yes, it stands out on your news feed. If you see that as your ad here, it's going to stand out, but then you read the copy and you've created dissidence because now all of a sudden it doesn't flow. It's like if you saw the alphabet and it said A, B, C, D, E, that's congruent, it said A, B, F, G, and wait, there's some letters missing, that doesn't make sense. So then you're starting to get people questioning. So that's a key thing about making sure you have congruence all the way through your funnel, and then ultimately you're feeding that all back. So once you've got your ad angle, so your ad angle comes from each of these. And you've built your copy, you can very quickly iterate that. So once you've got your headline here, you've got your image and you've got your body copy, you could create two, three, four, five different headlines. That's already five different ads. And then for each of those five ads, you could test different visuals. It could be static versus video, and it could be five different variants of the static or video thumbnail. And then you can have different variants of the body copy. So now all of a sudden you've already created 15, 20, 25 different ads that you can test right off the bat. So like that's the power of really thinking through your avatar and building that into your ad, into your offer. Now, one of the questions I get back is, does the offer, the term offer, need to have a discount? No, it doesn't. The offer is literally just a bridge between your product and audience. Doesn't mean you can't have an offer, and offers are best done when they complement your product. So for example, let's say here you're talking about the problem and solution, which is your dog choose furniture and here's a way to solve it. You could land them on the landing page, explain the product in more detail and say, hey, by the way, you can get, if you buy today, you can get three for the price of one or whatever the promotion is, just to stack up that value. Because again, you're working on value here. Everywhere you go, it's value and it's value exchange. If that's the value of what you're giving the user is far bigger than your commitment from them, then you'll get a transaction. So again, the value could simply be that this dog toy is $20. And for the benefit of not having to replace your sofa, it's probably worth it.

## [18:09 - 21:10]

That in itself is high value. If you say, for example, well, actually it's $20 plus you get XYZ, now you're stacking up the bundle and the value is increasing further as well. ## [21:10 - 23:45]

But when I look at their ad account, it's a mess. You know, there are gaps in terms of their excludes.
